titleStmt.title: ELLIPTIC GENERA AND SL(2, Z) MODULAR FORMS FOR FIBRE BUNDLES

analytic.title: ELLIPTIC GENERA AND SL(2, Z) MODULAR FORMS FOR FIBRE BUNDLES

application.desc: GROBID - A machine learning software for extracting information from scholarly documents
application.label: a91ee48

startPage=-1, endPage=-1, consolidateCitations=0, consolidateHeader=1, consolidateFunders=0, includeRawAffiliations=false, includeRawCitations=false, includeRawCopyrights=false, generateTeiIds=false, generateTeiCoordinates=[], sentenceSegmentation=false, flavor=null

div.p: By the family index theory, we generalize some well-known SL(2, Z) modular forms to the family case and obtain some new anomaly cancellation formulas for the determinant line bundle and index gerbes, and certain results about eta invariants. Moreover, for the higher degree case, we give some anomaly cancellation formulas of residue Chern forms.

div.head: Introduction
div.p: In [1], Alvarez-Gaumé and Witten discovered a formula that represents the beautiful relationship between the top components of the Hirzebruch L-form and A-form of a 12-dimensional smooth Riemannian manifold. This formula is called the "miracle cancellation" formula for gravitational anomalies. In [16], Liu established higher dimensional "miraculous cancellation" formulas for (8k + 4)-dimensional Riemannian manifolds by developing modular invariance properties of characteristic forms. These formulas could be used to deduce some divisibility results. In [11], Han, Liu and Zhang showed that both of the Green-Schwarz anomaly factorization formula for the gauge group E 8 × E 8 and the Horava-Witten anomaly factorization formula for the gauge group E 8 could be derived through modular forms of weight 14. This answered a question of J. H. Schwarz. They also established generalizations of these factorization formulas and obtaind a new Horava-Witten type factorization formula on 12-dimensional manifolds. In [12], Han, Huang, Liu and Zhang introduced a modular form of weight 14 over SL(2, Z) and a modular form of weight 10 over SL(2, Z) and they got some interesting anomaly cancellation formulas on 12-dimensional manifolds. In [17], a modular form of weight 2k for a 4k-dimensional spin manifold was introduced. In [6], Chen, Han and Zhang defined an integral modular form of weight 2k for a 4k-dimensional spin c manifold and an integral modular form of weight 2k for a 4k + 2-dimensional spin c manifold. In [21] and [22], we obtained some new anomaly cancellation formulas for spin manifolds and spinc manifolds and complex manifolds by studying above SL(2, Z) modular forms. Some divisibility results of the index of the twisted Dirac operator and spinc Dirac operators are obtained.

On the other hand, anomaly measures the nontriviality of the determinant line bundle of a family of Dirac operators. The perturbative anomaly detects the real first Chern class of the determinant line bundle while the global anomaly detects the integral first Chern class beyond the real information. For a family of Dirac operators on an evendimensional closed manifold, the determinant line bundle over the parametrizing space carries the Quillen metric as well as the Bismut-Freed connection compatible with the Quillen metric such that the curvature of the Bismut-Freed connection is the two-form component of the integration of the A-form of the vertical tangent bundle along the fiber in the Atiyah-Singer family index theorem [3,4]. In [18], for a family of Dirac operators on an odd-dimensional manifold, Lott constructs an abelian gerbe-with-connection whose curvature is the three-form component of the Atiyah-Singer family index theorem. This gerbe is called the index gerbe, which is a higher analogue of the determinant line bundle.

In [10], Han and Liu by combining modoular forms and characteristic forms, they obtained anomaly cancellation formulas for the determinant line bundle and derived anomaly cancellation formulas for index gerbes and obtained certain results about eta invariants. In [19], Mickelsson and Paycha provided local expressions for Chern-Weil forms built from superconnections associated with families of Dirac operators previously investigated by Paycha and Scott. They interpreted the even and odd higher degree Chern-Weil type forms built from superconnection as the Wodzicki residue. The purpose of this paper is to generalize some well-known SL(2, Z) modular forms to the family case and obtain some new anomaly cancellation formulas for the determinant line bundle and index gerbes, and certain results about eta invariants as in [10]. Moreover, for the higher degree case, we will give some anomaly cancellation formulas of residue Chern forms. The structure of this paper is briefly described below: In Section 2, we generalize the results in [22] to the family case and obtain some new anomaly cancellation formulas for the determinant line bundle and index gerbes, and certain results about eta invariants. In Section 3, we generalize the results in [21] to the family case and define the family two variable elliptic genus and get some formulas for the determinant line bundle and index gerbes, and certain results about eta invariants. In Section 4, for the higher degree case, we will give some anomaly cancellation formulas of residue Chern forms by the Mickelsson and Paycha's theorem .

div.head: Anomaly cancellation formulas and SL(2, Z) modular forms for fibre bundles
div.p: Let M be a closed manifold and M → B be a fibre bundle with the closed spin fibre Z. Let T Z be the vertical bundle and △(T Z) be the vertical spinor bundle. Set
div.formula: (2.1) Θ 1 (T C Z) = ∞ n=1 S q n ( T C Z) ⊗ ∞ m=1 ∧ q m ( T C Z), (2.2) Θ 2 (T C Z) = ∞ n=1 S q n ( T C Z) ⊗ ∞ m=1 ∧ -q m-1 (2.3) Θ 3 (T C Z) = ∞ n=1 S q n ( T C Z) ⊗ ∞ m=1 ∧ q m-1 2 ( T C Z).
div.p: Let dimT Z = 4k -2 and

We know that the coefficient 2 2k-1 is different from the coefficient 2 2k in [22] which makes the coefficients of our cancellation formulas are different from formulas in [22]. We first recall the four Jacobi theta functions are defined as follows( cf. [5]):
where q = e 2π √ -1τ with τ ∈ H, the upper half complex plane. One has the following transformation laws of theta functions under the actions of S and T (cf. [5] ):
Definition 2.1. A modular form over SL(2, Z) is a holomorphic function f (τ ) on H such that (2.14) f (gτ
div.formula: ) := f aτ + b cτ + d = (cτ + d) l f (τ ), ∀g = a b c d ∈ SL(2, Z), l is called the weight of f . Theorem 2.2. Q(T Z, τ
div.p: ) is a modular form over SL 2 (Z) with the weight 2k.
Proof. Let {±2π √ -1x j } (1 ≤ j ≤ 2k -1) be the formal Chern roots for T C Z, then we have
div.formula: Q(T Z, τ + 1) = Q(T Z, τ ) and Q(T Z, -1 τ ) = τ 2k Q(T Z, τ ), so Q(T Z, τ
div.p: ) is a modular form over SL 2 (Z) with the weight 2k. □
We expand Q(T Z, τ ) as follows:
div.formula: Q(T Z, τ ) = A(T Z)ch(△(T Z)) + 2 2k A(T Z)(4k)
+ 2 A(T Z)ch(△(T Z))ch( T C Z) + 2 2k A(T Z)ch( T C Z + ∧ 2 T C Z) (4k) q + A(T Z)ch(△(T Z))ch(A 0 ) + 2 2k A(T Z)ch(A 1 )(4k)
div.p: where
div.formula: A 0 = 2 T C Z + ∧ 2 T C Z + T C Z ⊗ T C Z + S 2 T C Z, (2.17) A 1 = ∧ 4 T C Z + ∧ 2 T C Z ⊗ T C Z + T C Z ⊗ T C Z + S 2 T C Z + T C Z.
div.p: Let L D Z ⊗V = det(Ker(D Z ⊗V ) + ) * ⊗det(Ker(D Z ⊗V ) -) be the determinant line bundle of the family operator D Z ⊗V over Z (for associated definitions, see [3], [4]). The determinant line bundle carries the Quillen metric g L D Z ⊗V as well as the Bismut-Freed connection ∇ L D Z ⊗V compatible to g L D Z ⊗V , the curvature R L D Z ⊗V of which is equal to the two-form component of the integration of A(T Z, ∇ T Z )ch(V, ∇ V ) along the fibre in the Atiyah-Singer family index theorem [3,4].
div.formula: √ -1/(2π)R L D Z ⊗V is a representative of the local anomaly. Theorem 2.3. (Bismut and Freed [4]) (2.18) R L D Z ⊗V = 2π √ -1 Z A(T Z, ∇ Z )ch(V, ∇ V )(2)
div.p: The Bismut-Freed theorem is correct for the spinc case.
Theorem 2.4. When dimT Z = 6, we have
div.formula: R L D Z ⊗△(T Z)⊗ T C Z + 8R L D Z ⊗( T C Z+∧ 2 T C Z) = 120(R L D Z ⊗△(T Z) + 16R L D Z ), (2.19) R L D Z ⊗△(T Z)⊗A 0 + 16R L D Z ⊗A 1 = 2160(R L D Z ⊗△(T Z) + 16R L D Z ). (2.20)
div.p: The proof of Theorem 2.4: It is well known that modular forms over SL 2 (Z) can be expressed as polynomials of the Einsentein series E 4 (τ ) and E 6 (τ ), where
Their weights are 4 and 6 respectively. When k = 2, then Q(T Z, τ ) is a modular form over SL 2 (Z) with the weight 4. Q(T Z, τ ) must be a multiple of
By Theorem 2.2 and 2.3 and (2.16-2.17), we have Theorem 2.4. Similarly to Theorem 2.4, by the method in [22], we have Theorem 2.5. When dimT Z = 10, we have
div.formula: R L D Z ⊗△(T Z)⊗ T C Z + 32R L D Z ⊗( T C Z+∧ 2 T C Z) = -252(R L D Z ⊗△(T Z) + 64R L D Z ), (2.24) R L D Z ⊗△(T Z)⊗A 0 + 64R L D Z ⊗A 1 = -16632(R L D Z ⊗△(T Z) + 64R L D Z ). (2.25) Theorem 2.6. When dimT Z = 14, we have R L D Z ⊗△(T Z)⊗ T C Z + 128R L D Z ⊗( T C Z+∧ 2 T C Z) = 240(R L D Z ⊗△(T Z) + 256R L D Z ), (2.26) R L D Z ⊗△(T Z)⊗A 0 + 256R L D Z ⊗A 1 = 61920(R L D Z ⊗△(T Z) + 256R L D Z ). (2.27) Theorem 2.7. When dimT Z = 18, we have R L D Z ⊗△(T Z)⊗ T C Z + 512R L D Z ⊗( T C Z+∧ 2 T C Z) = -132(R L D Z ⊗△(T Z) + 1024R L D Z ), (2.28) R L D Z ⊗△(T Z)⊗A 0 + 1024R L D Z ⊗A 1 = -117288(R L D Z ⊗△(T Z) + 1024R L D Z ). (2.29) Let dimT Z = 4k -3 and Q(T Z, τ ) = [ A(T Z)[ch(△(T Z) ⊗ Θ 1 (T C Z)) + 2 2k-2 Θ 2 (T C Z) + 2 2k-2 Θ 3 (T C Z)] (4k) . (2.30) Theorem 2.8. Q(T Z, τ ) is a modular form over SL 2 (Z) with the weight 2k.
div.p: We expand Q(T Z, τ ) as follows:
div.formula: Q(T Z, τ ) = A(T Z)ch(△(T Z)) + 2 2k-1 A(T Z) (4k) (2.31) + 2 A(T Z)ch(△(T Z))ch( T C Z) + 2 2k-1 A(T Z)ch( T C Z + ∧ 2 T C Z) (4k) q + A(T Z)ch(△(T Z))ch(A 0 ) + 2 2k-1 A(T Z)ch(A 1 ) (4k) q 2 + • • • .
div.p: Below we consider anomaly cancellation formulas of index gerbes. Now we still assume that T Z is oriented but the dimension of the fibre is 4k -3, i.e. we consider odd-dimensional fibres. Let T Z is spin. One can still define the family of Dirac operator D Z ⊗ V similar as the even-dimensional fibre case. The only difference is that now the spinor bundle
div.formula: △(T Z) associated to T Z is not Z 2 -graded. Let H ∞ be the set of C ∞ sections of △(T Z) ⊗ V over M . H ∞ is viewed as the set of C ∞ sections over B of infinite-dimensional bundles which are still denoted by H ∞ . For b ∈ B, H ∞ b is the set of C ∞ sections over Z b of △(T Z) ⊗ V . For each b ∈ B, (D Z ⊗ V ) b ∈ End(H ∞ b ) is the twisted Dirac operator on the fibre Z b .
div.p: As a higher analogue of the determinant line bundle, Lott [18] constructs the index gerbe G D Z ⊗V with connection on B for the family twisted odd Dirac operator D Z ⊗ V , the curvature R G D Z ⊗V (a closed 3-form on B) of which is equal to the three-form component of the integration of A(T Z, ∇ Z )ch(V, ∇ V ) along the fibre in the Atiyah-Singer families index theorem. Theorem 2.9. (Lott [18])
div.formula: (2.32) R G D Z ⊗V = Z A(T Z, ∇ Z )ch(V, ∇ V )(3)
div.p: Similarly to Theorem 2.4-2.7, We have the following anomaly cancellation formulas for index gerbes.
Theorem 2.10. When dimT Z = 5, we have
div.formula: R G D Z ⊗△(T Z)⊗ T C Z + 4R L D Z ⊗( T C Z+∧ 2 T C Z) = 120(R G D Z ⊗△(T Z) + 8G L D Z ), (2.33) R G D Z ⊗△(T Z)⊗A 0 + 8R L D Z ⊗A 1 = 2160(R G D Z ⊗△(T Z) + 8R G D Z ). (2.34) Theorem 2.11. When dimT Z = 9, we have R G D Z ⊗△(T Z)⊗ T C Z + 16R L D Z ⊗( T C Z+∧ 2 T C Z) = -252(R G D Z ⊗△(T Z) + 32G L D Z ), (2.35) R G D Z ⊗△(T Z)⊗A 0 + 32R L D Z ⊗A 1 = -16632(R G D Z ⊗△(T Z) + 32R G D Z ). (2.36) Theorem 2.12. When dimT Z = 13, we have R G D Z ⊗△(T Z)⊗ T C Z + 64R L D Z ⊗( T C Z+∧ 2 T C Z) = 240(R G D Z ⊗△(T Z) + 128G L D Z ), (2.37) R G D Z ⊗△(T Z)⊗A 0 + 128R L D Z ⊗A 1 = 61920(R G D Z ⊗△(T Z) + 128R G D Z ). (2.38) Theorem 2.13. When dimT Z = 17, we have R G D Z ⊗△(T Z)⊗ T C Z + 256R L D Z ⊗( T C Z+∧ 2 T C Z) = -132(R G D Z ⊗△(T Z) + 512G L D Z ), (2.39) R G D Z ⊗△(T Z)⊗A 0 + 512R L D Z ⊗A 1 = -117288(R G D Z ⊗△(T Z) + 512R G D Z ). (2.40)
div.p: Let dimT Z = 4k -1 and define Q(T Z, τ ) which has same expression and expansion as Q(T Z, τ ). Q(T Z, τ ) is a modular form over SL 2 (Z) with the weight 2k. Below we consider η-invariants.
For b ∈ B, let η b (D Z ⊗ V )(s) be the eta function associated with (D Z ⊗ V ) b . Define as Atiyah, Patodi and Singer in [2] ηb
div.formula: (D Z ⊗ V )(s) = η b (D Z ⊗ V )(s) + ker(D Z ⊗ V ) b 2 . Denote ηb (D Z ⊗ V )(0) (a function on B) by η(D Z ⊗ V ).
div.p: We still adopt the setting of family twisted Dirac operators on a family of odd-dimensional manifolds in index gerbes. For odd-dimensional fibres, we have the following Bismut-Freed theorem for the reduced η-invariants.
Theorem 2.14. (Bismut and Freed [4])
We have the following theorems on the reduced η-invariants.
Theorem 2.15. When dimT Z = 7, we have
(2.43) Theorem 2.16. When dimT Z = 11, we have
(2.45) Theorem 2.17. When dimT Z = 15, we have
(2.47) Theorem 2.18. When dimT Z = 19, we have

Let T Z be spin c and L be the complex line bundle associated to the given spin c structure on T Z. Denote by c = c 1 (L) the first Chern class of L. Also, we use L R for the notation of L, when it is viewed as an oriented real plane bundle. Let Θ(T C Z, L R ⊗ C) be the virtual complex vector bundle over M defined by
div.formula: Θ(T C M, L R ⊗ C) = ∞ n=1 S q n ( T C M ) ⊗ ∞ m=1 ∧ q m ( L R ⊗ C) ⊗ ∞ r=1 ∧ -q r-1 2 ( L R ⊗ C) ⊗ ∞ s=1 ∧ q s-1 2 ( L R ⊗ C), Θ * (T C M, L R ⊗ C) = ∞ n=1 S q n ( T C M ) ⊗ ∞ m=1 ∧ -q m ( L R ⊗ C).
div.p: For dimT Z = 4k -3, 4k -2, 4k -1 three cases, we always let
div.formula: (2.50) Q(T Z, L, τ ) = { A(T Z, ∇ T Z )exp( c 2 )ch(Θ(T C Z, L R ⊗ C))} (4k) , (2.51) Q * (T Z, L, τ ) = { A(T Z, ∇ T Z )exp( c 2 )ch(Θ * (T C Z, L R ⊗ C))} (4k+2) . Proposition 2.19. When p 1 (T Z) = 3p 1 (L R ),then Q(T Z, L, τ ) is a modular form over SL 2 (Z) with the weight 2k. When p 1 (T Z) = p 1 (L R ),then Q * (T Z, L, τ
div.p: ) is a modular form over SL 2 (Z) with the weight 2k.
We have
div.formula: Q(T Z, L, τ ) = A(T Z)exp( c2
div.p: where
div.formula: B 1 = T C Z + 2 ∧ 2 L R,C -L R,C ⊗ L R,C + L R,C ,(2.53)

B 2 = S 2 T C Z + T C Z + (2 ∧ 2 L R,C -L R,C ⊗ L R,C + L R,C ) ⊗ T C Z + ∧ 2 L R,C ⊗ ∧ 2 L R,C + 2 ∧ 4 L R,C -2 L R,C ⊗ ∧ 3 L R,C + 2 L R,C ⊗ ∧ 2 L R,C -L R,C ⊗ L R,C ⊗ L R,C + L R,C + ∧ 2 L R,C . Q * (T Z, L, τ ) = A(T Z)exp( c2
div.p: where
div.formula: B 3 = T C Z -L R,C , (2.55) B 4 = ∧ 2 L R,C -L R,C -L R,C ⊗ T C Z + S 2 T C Z + T C Z.
div.p: Denote the spinc Dirac operator by D c . Similarly to previous theorems, we have Theorem 2.20. When dimT Z = 6 and p 1 (T Z) = 3p 1 (L R ), we have
div.formula: R L D Z c ⊗B 1 = 240R L D Z c , (2.56) R L D Z c ⊗B 2 = 2160R L D Z c . When dimT Z = 6 and p 1 (T Z) = p 1 (L R ), we have R L D Z c ⊗B 3 = 240R L D Z c , (2.57) R L D Z c ⊗B 4 = 2160R L D Z c .
div.p: Theorem 2.21. When dimT Z = 5 and p 1 (T Z) = 3p 1 (L R ), we have
div.formula: R G D Z c ⊗B 1 = 240R G D Z c , (2.58) R G D Z c ⊗B 2 = 2160R G D Z c . When dimT Z = 5 and p 1 (T Z) = p 1 (L R ), we have R G D Z c ⊗B 3 = 240R G D Z c , (2.59) R G D Z c ⊗B 4 = 2160R G D Z c . Theorem 2.22. When dimT Z = 7 and p 1 (T Z) = 3p 1 (L R ), we have η(D Z c ⊗ B 1 ) = 240η(D Z c ) + c 1 , (2.60) η(D Z c ⊗ B 2 ) = 2160η(D Z c ) + c 2 . When dimT Z = 7 and p 1 (T Z) = p 1 (L R ), we have η(D Z c ⊗ B 3 ) = 240η(D Z c ) + c 3 , (2.61) η(D Z c ⊗ B 4 ) = 2160η(D Z c ) + c 4 . Theorem 2.23. When dimT Z = 10 and p 1 (T Z) = 3p 1 (L R ), we have R L D Z c ⊗B 1 = -504R L D Z c , (2.62) R L D Z c ⊗B 2 = -16632R L D Z c . YONG WANG When dimT Z = and p 1 (T Z) = p 1 (L R ), we have R L D Z c ⊗B 3 = -504R L D Z c , (2.63) R L D Z c ⊗B 4 = -16632R L D Z c .
div.p: Theorem 2.24. When dimT Z = 9 and p 1 (T Z) = 3p 1 (L R ), we have
div.formula: R G D Z c ⊗B 1 = -504R G D Z c , (2.64) R G D Z c ⊗B 2 = -16632R G D Z c . When dimT Z = and p 1 (T Z) = p 1 (L R ), we have R G D Z c ⊗B 3 = -504R G D Z c , (2.65) R G D Z c ⊗B 4 = -16632R G D Z c . Theorem 2.25. When dimT Z = 11 and p 1 (T Z) = 3p 1 (L R ), we have η(D Z c ⊗ B 1 ) = -504η(D Z c ) + c 5 , (2.66) η(D Z c ⊗ B 2 ) = -16632η(D Z c ) + c 6 . When dimT Z = and p 1 (T Z) = p 1 (L R ), we have η(D Z c ⊗ B 3 ) = -504η(D Z c ) + c 7 , (2.67) η(D Z c ⊗ B 4 ) = -16632η(D Z c ) + c 8 . Theorem 2.26. When dimT Z = 14 and p 1 (T Z) = 3p 1 (L R ), we have R L D Z c ⊗B 1 = 480R L D Z c , (2.68) R L D Z c ⊗B 2 = 61920R L D Z c . When dimT Z = and p 1 (T Z) = p 1 (L R ), we have R L D Z c ⊗B 3 = 480R L D Z c , (2.69) R L D Z c ⊗B 4 = 61920R L D Z c . Theorem 2.27. When dimT Z = 13 and p 1 (T Z) = 3p 1 (L R ), we have R G D Z c ⊗B 1 = 480R G D Z c , (2.70) R G D Z c ⊗B 2 = 61920R G D Z c . When dimT Z = and p 1 (T Z) = p 1 (L R ), we have R G D Z c ⊗B 3 = 480R G D Z c , (2.71) R G D Z c ⊗B 4 = 61920R G D Z c .

When dimT Z = 15 and p 1 (T Z) = p 1 (L R ), we have
Theorem 2.29. When dimT Z = 18 and p 1 (T Z) = 3p 1 (L R ), we have
div.formula: R L D Z c ⊗B 1 = -264R L D Z c , (2.74) R L D Z c ⊗B 2 = -117288R L D Z c . When dimT Z = 18 and p 1 (T Z) = p 1 (L R ), we have R L D Z c ⊗B 3 = -264R L D Z c , (2.75) R L D Z c ⊗B 4 = -117288R L D Z c .

div.formula: R G D Z c ⊗B 1 = -264R G D Z c , (2.76) R G D Z c ⊗B 2 = -117288R G D Z c . When dimT Z = 17 and p 1 (T Z) = p 1 (L R ), we have R G D Z c ⊗B 3 = -264R G D Z c , (2.77) R G D Z c ⊗B 4 = -117288R G D Z c . Theorem 2.31. When dimT Z = 19 and p 1 (T Z) = 3p 1 (L R ), we have η(D Z c ⊗ B 1 ) = -264η(D Z c ) + c 13 , (2.78) η(D Z c ⊗ B 2 ) = -117288η(D Z c ) + c 14 .
div.p: When dimT Z = 19 and p 1 (T Z) = p 1 (L R ), we have
where c j , 1 ≤ j ≤ 16 is a constant.

div.head: The two-variable elliptic genus for fibre bundles
div.p: Let (T Z, J) be a 2d -2-dimensional vector bundle with the almost complex structure J and T 1,0 Z be the holomorphic tangent bundle in the sense of J and T 1,0 * Z is the dual of T 1,0 Z. Let W denote a complex l-dimensional vector bundle on M . Denote the first Chern classes of T 1,0 Z and W by c 1 (T Z) and c 1 (W ). We denote by 2π and 2π √ -1w j (1 ≤ j ≤ l) respectively the formal Chern roots of T 1,0 Z and W . Then the Todd form of T Z is defined by
div.formula: (3.1) Td(T Z) := d-1 i=1 2π √ -1x i 1 -e -2π √ -1x i . Let (τ, z) ∈ H × C
div.p: where H is the upper half plane and C is the complex plane. Let y = e 2π √ -1z and q = e 2π √ -1τ and c := ∞ j=1 (1 -q j ).
Let

where the coefficient c 2(d-1-l) is different from the coefficient c 2(d-l) in [15] Definition 3.1. The two variable elliptic genus of M → B with respect to W , which we denote by Ell(T Z, W, τ, z) is defined by
div.formula: (3.3) Ell(T Z, W, τ, z) := exp( c 1 (W ) -c 1 (T 1,0 Z) 2 )T d(T Z)ch(E(T M, W, τ, z))(2d)
div.p: , Using the same calculations as in Lemma 3.4 in [15] and we have Lemma 3.2.
div.formula: Ell(M, W, τ, z) = η(τ ) 3(d-1-l) d-1 i=1 2π √ -1x i θ(τ, x i ) l j=1 θ(τ, w j -z) (2d) , (3.4)
div.p: where

Similarly to Theorem 3. Similar to Proposition 3.6 in [15] (our coefficients of a n are different from the coefficients in [15]) , we have Proposition 3.4. Let c 1 (W ) = c 1 (T 1,0 Z) = 0 and the first Pontrjagin classes p 1 (M ) = p 1 (W ), then the series a n (T Z, W, τ ) determined by
div.formula: exp(-4π 2 lE 2 (τ )z 2 )Ell(T Z, W, τ, z) = n≥0 a n (T Z, W, τ ) • z n , (3.6)
div.p: are modular forms of weight d -l + n over SL(2, Z). Furthermore, the first five series of a n (T Z, W, τ ) are of the following form:
where
and
where
where
where
Similarly, by a 1 (T Z, W, τ ), we have
div.formula: Theorem 3.7. Let dimT Z = 2d -2, c 1 (W ) = c 1 (T 1,0 Z) = 0 and the first Pontrjagin classes p 1 (T Z) = p 1 (W ), then 1)if d -l = 3, then R L D Z ⊗∧ -1 W * ⊗A 4 = 240R L D Z ⊗( l p=0 (-1) p (p-l 2 )∧ p W * ) , (3.25) 2)if d -l = 5, then R L D Z ⊗∧ -1 W * ⊗A 4 = -504R L D Z ⊗( l p=0 (-1) p (p-l 2 )∧ p W * ) , (3.26) 3)if d -l = 7, then R L D Z ⊗∧ -1 W * ⊗A 4 = 480R L D Z ⊗( l p=0 (-1) p (p-l 2 )∧ p W * ) , (3.27) 4)if d -l = 9, then R L D Z ⊗∧ -1 W * ⊗A 4 = -264R L D Z ⊗( l p=0 (-1) p (p-l 2 )∧ p W * ) , (3.28) Let T Z be a 2d -3-dimensional spin vector bundle. Let ±2π √ -1x i (1 ≤ i ≤ d -2), 0 and 2π √ -1w j (1 ≤ j ≤ l) respectively the formal Chern roots of T C Z and W . Then Let E(T Z, W, τ, z) :=c 2(d-l)-3 y -l 2 ∞ n=1 (∧ -yq n-1 (W * ) ⊗ ∧ -y -1 q n (W )) (3.29) ∞ n=1 S q n (T C Z) ,
div.p: where the coefficient c 2(d-1)-3 is different from the coefficient c 2(d-l)-2 in dimT Z = 2d -2.
Definition 3.8. The two variable elliptic genus of M → B with respect to W , which we denote by Ell(T Z, W, τ, z) is defined by
div.formula: (3.30) Ell(T Z, W, τ, z) := exp( c 1 (W ) 2 ) A(T Z)ch( E(T Z, W, τ, z))(2d)
div.p: Using the same calculations as in Lemma 3.4 in [15] and we have Lemma 3.9.
div.formula: Ell(T Z, W, τ, z) = η(τ ) 3(d-l-2) d-2 i=1 2π √ -1x i θ(τ, x i ) l j=1 θ(τ, w j -z)
div.p: where
Similarly, by a 1 (T Z, W, τ ), we have Theorem 3.14. Let dimT Z = 2d -3, c 1 (W ) = 0 and the first Pontrjagin classes
div.formula: p 1 (T Z) = p 1 (W ), then 1)if d -l = 3, then R G D Z ⊗∧ -1 W * ⊗A 10 = 240R G D Z ⊗( l p=0 (-1) p (p-l 2 )∧ p W * ) , (3.50) 2)if d -l = 5, then R G D Z ⊗∧ -1 W * ⊗A 10 = -504R G D Z ⊗( l p=0 (-1) p (p-l 2 )∧ p W * ) , (3.51) 3)if d -l = 7, then R G D Z ⊗∧ -1 W * ⊗A 10 = 480R G D Z ⊗( l p=0 (-1) p (p-l 2 )∧ p W * ) , (3.52) 4)if d -l = 9, then R G D Z ⊗∧ -1 W * ⊗A 10 = -264R G D Z ⊗( l p=0 (-1) p (p-l 2 )∧ p W * ) , (3.53) Let T Z be a 2d -1-dimensional spin vector bundle. Then Let E(T Z, W, τ, z) :=c 2(d-l)-1 y -l 2 ∞ n=1 (∧ -yq n-1 (W * ) ⊗ ∧ -y -1 q n (W )) (3.54) ∞ n=1 S q n (T C Z) ,
div.p: Similarly to the definition Ell(T Z, W, τ, z), we can define the two variable elliptic genus Ell(T Z, W, τ, z). Using the same calculations as in Lemma 3.4 in [15] and we have Lemma 3.15.
div.formula: Ell(T Z, W, τ, z) = η(τ ) 3(d-l-1) d-1 i=1 2π √ -1x i θ(τ, x i ) l j=1 θ(τ, w j -z) (2d) , (3
(-4π 2 lE 2 (τ )z 2 )Ell(T Z, W, τ, z) = n≥0 a n (T Z, W, τ ) • z n , (3.56)
div.p: are modular forms of weight d -l + n over SL(2, Z). Furthermore, the first five series of a n (M, W, g, τ ) are of the following form:
where
where

+ O(q), (3.62) ). Let dimZ = 2r + 1 and r + j be even and define Q 2 (T Z, τ ) by the same expression of Q 1 (T Z, τ ). Q 2 (T Z, τ ) is a modular form over SL 2 (Z) with the weight (r + j) and Q 1 (T Z, τ ) has the same expansion of Q 1 (T Z, τ ). . Remark 1. We can use Theorem 4.1 to give the higher degree cases of Theorems in previous sections. We also may generalize the results in [9] to family cases using the tricks in this paper. But we do not know whether one has the similar theorems due to
div.formula: (T Z) = p 1 (W ), then 1)if either d -l is odd or d -l ≤ 2 but d -l ̸ = 0, then η(D Z ⊗ ∧ -1 W * ) = c 1 , η(D Z ⊗ ∧ -1 W * ⊗ A 11 ) = c 2 , (3.65) η(D Z ⊗ ∧ -1 W * ⊗ A 12 ) = c 3 , 2)if either d -l is even or d -l ≤ 1 but d -l ̸ = -1, then η(D Z ⊗ ( l p=0 (-1) p (p - l 2 ) ∧ p W * )) = c 4 , (3.66) η(D Z ⊗ A 13 ) = c 5 , 3)if either d -l is odd or d -l ≤ 0 but d -l ̸ = -2, then η(D Z ⊗ A 5 ) = c 6 , (3

figure.figDesc: 2 in[15], we have Theorem 3.3. If c 1 (W ) = c 1 (T 1,0 Z) = 0 and the first Pontrjagin classes p 1 (T Z) = p 1 (W ), then the elliptic genus Ell(T Z, W, τ, z) is a weak Jacobi form of weight d -l and index l 2 .

to Theorem 3.2 in[15], we have Lemma 3.10. If c 1 (W ) = 0 and the first Pontrjagin classes p 1 (T Z) = p 1 (W ), then the elliptic genus Ell(T Z, W, τ, z) is a weak Jacobi form of weight d -l and index l 2 . Similar to Proposition 3.6 in [15] (our coefficients of a n are different from the coefficients in a n ) , we have Proposition 3.11. If c 1 (W ) = 0 and the first Pontrjagin classes p 1 (T Z) = p 1 (W ), then the series a n (T Z, W, τ ) determined by exp(-4π 2 lE 2 (τ )z 2 ) Ell(T Z, W, τ, z) = n≥0 a n (T Z, W, τ ) • z n , (3.32) are modular forms of weight d -l + n over SL(2, Z). Furthermore, the first five series of a n (M, W, g, τ ) are of the following form:

.55) Similarly to Theorem 3.2 in [15], we have Lemma 3.16. If c 1 (W ) = 0 and the first Pontrjagin classes p 1 (T Z) = p 1 (W ), then the elliptic genus Ell(T Z, W, τ, z) is a weak Jacobi form of weight d -l and index l 2 . Proposition 3.17. If dimT Z = 2d -1, c 1 (W ) = 0 and the first Pontrjagin classes p 1 (T Z) = p 1 (W ), then the series a n (T Z, W, τ ) determined by exp
(3.64) Since there are no SL(2, Z) modular forms with the odd weight or the non zero weight ≤ 2, we have Proposition 3.18. Let dimT Z = 2d -1, c 1 (W ) = 0 and the first Pontrjagin classes p 1

.67)4)if either d -l is even or d -l ≤ -1 but d -l ̸ = -3, then η(D Z ⊗ A 6 ) = c 7 , (3.68) 5)if either d -l is odd or d -l ≤ -2 but d -l ̸ = -4, then η(D Z ⊗ A 7 ) = c 8 , (3.69) where c j , 1 ≤ j ≤ 8 is a constant. Theorem 3.19. Let dimT Z = 2d -1, c 1 (W ) = 0 and the first Pontrjagin classes p 1 (T Z) = p 1 (W ), then 1)if d -l = 4, then η(D Z ⊗ ∧ -1 W * ⊗ A 11 ) = 240η(D Z ⊗ ∧ -1 W * ), (3.70) η(D Z ⊗ ∧ -1 W * ⊗ A 12 ) = 2160η(D Z ⊗ ∧ -1 W * ).

div.p: where

Since there are no SL(2, Z) modular forms with the odd weight or the non zero weight ≤ 2, we have Proposition 3.5. Let dimT Z = 2d -2, c 1 (W ) = c 1 (T 1,0 Z) = 0 and the first Pontrjagin classes p 1 (T Z) = p 1 (W ), then 1)if either d -l is odd or d -l ≤ 2 but d -l ̸ = 0, then
where
Since there are no SL(2, Z) modular forms with the odd weight or the non zero weight ≤ 2, we have Proposition 3.12.
Similarly, by a 1 (T Z, W, τ ), we have

div.head: The higher degree case
div.p: Firstly, we recall the main theorem in [19].

Theorem 4.1. ( [19]) Let M → B be a closed fibre bundle with the spin closed fibre Z and A V be the Bismut superconnection twisted by the vector bundle V . When dimZ = 2r, for the j-th Chern form str(A 2j
V ) [2j] , we have
When dimZ = 2r + 1, for the j-th residue Chern form sres(|A| 2j-1
where the definitions of the j-th Chern form str(A 2j
V ) [2j] and the j-th residue Chern form sres(|A| 2j-1 V ) [2j-1] can be founded in [19] and [20] and n is the dimension of the fibre.
Let dimZ = 2r and r + j be even and
) is a modular form over SL 2 (Z) with the weight (r+j) and We expand Q 1 (T Z, τ ) as follows:
When r + j = 6, we have
When r + j = 8, we have

Bismut-Freed and Lott for the Dai-Zhang's Toeplitz family index theorem ( [7]) or not. We can also give the higher degree cases of [10] and [8]. Thus by the Lott's theorem about the curvature of the Deligne cohomology class in [18], we may give another higher degree explanation.

Remark 2. Motivated by Theorem 4.1, it is interesting to extend the well-known Kaster-Kalau-Walze theorem in NCG ( [13], [14]) to the family case. That is to compute the family Wodzicki residue of |A| -n+2 on the fibration M with n-dimensional fibre Z.

div.head: Acknowledgements
div.p: The author was supported by Science and Technology Development Plan Project of Jilin Province, China: No.20260102245JC.

div.head: Data availability
div.p: No data was gathered for this article.

div.head: Conflict of interest
div.p: The authors have no relevant financial or non-financial interests to disclose.

analytic.title: Graviational anomalies
Spectral asymmetry and Riemannian geometry I
The analysis of elliptic families, I: Metrics and connections on determinant bundles
The analysis of elliptic families, II: Dirac operators, eta invariants, and the holonomy theorem
Generalized Witten genus and vanishing theorems
Higher spectral flow
Modular invariance and anomaly cancellation formulas for fiber bundles
SL(2, Z) modular forms and Witten genus in odd dimensions
Gravitational anomaly cancellation and modular invariance
Anomaly cancellation and modularity
Cubic forms, anomaly cancellation and modularity
The Dirac Operator and Gravitation
Gravity, Noncommutative geometry and the Wodzicki residue
1-Phenomena for the pluri χ y -genus and elliptic genus
Modular invariance and characteristic numbers
On elliptic genera and theta-functions
Higher-degree analogs of the determinant line bundle
Renormalised Chern-Weil forms associated with families of Dirac operators
Chern-Weil forms associated with superconnections
SL(2, Z) modular forms and anomaly cancellation formulas for almost complex manifolds

monogr.title: SL(2, Z) modular forms and anomaly cancellation formulas
Lectures on Chern-weil Theory and Witten Deformations. Nankai Tracks in Mathematics
